zen_atelier.configuration.title: Zen Atelier Configs
patchouli.zen_atelier.room_journal.name: Room Journal
patchouli.zen_atelier.room_journal.landi: Rooms remember purpose. This journal keeps notes on anchors, comfort, craft, and the quiet habits that make a place useful.
patchouli.zen_atelier.room_journal.categ: How rooms are noticed, named, and improved.
Room types that can be recognized from anchors, furniture, and structure.
patchouli.zen_atelier.room_journal.entry: Rooms Remember Purpose
A room is more than walls. A bed, a lectern, a brewing stand, or a cauldron can give a place a clear purpose.
The room around that anchor matters too. Light, space, matching furniture, and a clear use make a room stronger.
Anchors and Names
Anchors are the objects a room gathers around. A bed remembers rest. A brewing stand remembers work. A cauldron changes an ordinary workroom into an atelier.
The journal can inspect a room when used carefully on an anchor. It shows the room's name, type, score, and any special notes the room has earned.
Nameplates and Epithets
A sign placed near an anchor can become that room's nameplate. A blank sign adopts the current room name; a written sign gives the room a custom name.
Some rooms earn rare epithets when their purpose and details line up especially well. The practical room type remains underneath, but the discovered name is the one the room remembers.
Bedrooms
A bedroom begins with a bed, but comfort comes from the room around it. Light, space, storage, carpet, and a calm material palette all help.
A cramped or cluttered bedroom still counts as a bedroom. It simply may not be a very restful one.
Churches and Ateliers
A village church may keep a brewing stand for a cleric. It is useful, but it is not the same as a proper atelier.
An atelier needs both a brewing stand and a cauldron. Plants, pots, light, and careful furnishing make the room feel more complete.
Village Workrooms
Village work buildings are good places to study room purpose. A stonecutter suggests masonry, a loom suggests clothwork, and a lectern suggests a library.
The strongest rooms keep one clear job. Mixed rooms can still work, but the room may need better signs, storage, or furnishing to make its purpose obvious.
Smithies
A smithy is built around hot work and gear repair. An anvil, grindstone, smithing table, or blast furnace can all suggest the room's purpose.
Storage, stone, metal, and controlled heat make a smithy easier to read. Too much plant life or bedroom furniture pulls the room in another direction.
Libraries
A library grows from bookshelves, chiseled bookshelves, and lecterns. It can stand alone or support an enchanting room.
Quiet rooms with clear walls, good light, and enough shelves make better libraries than a single lectern in a busy workshop.
Greenhouses
A greenhouse needs plants and enough glass to feel protected from the outside world.
Water, sunlight, glass, and plant variety all help the room feel like a place made for growing.
Kitchens
A kitchen is a utility room for food, heat, and preparation. Smokers, furnaces, campfires, storage, and water access all help define it.
Kitchens can overlap with storage, but they make poor bedrooms. Heat and work clutter tend to make rest worse.
Storage Rooms
A storage room is strongest when containers are grouped with a clear purpose and enough space to reach them.
A few chests can support another room. Many chests can become the room's main identity.
Enchanting Rooms
An enchanting room centers on a table of magic, surrounded by the knowledge needed to understand it.
Bookshelves amplify enchantment power. A room with both a table and many books becomes a true place of magical study.
Masonry Shops
A mason works stone into shape. A stonecutter is the heart of a masonry shop, but storage and stone materials make it complete.
Masonry complements building, but too many other work types in the same room dilute its purpose.
Map Rooms
A map room is for careful planning and knowledge. A cartography table, reference books, and clear workspace define it.
Maps, charts, and planning tools help a room feel purposeful. Good lighting and quiet make it a better place to think.
Loom Rooms
A loom room is built for textile work. A loom is essential, but carpet, storage, and fine materials help too.
Textiles need space to work and display. A well-organized loom room shows care in every detail.
Fletcheries
A fletchery is for making and maintaining ranged equipment. A fletching table, storage, and tools define the craft.
Arrows, bows, and careful organization make a fletchery feel like a place of precision work.
Gardener's Sheds
A gardener's shed is a workspace for cultivating plants. A composter, pots, plants, and soil make it real.
Plant variety and careful gardening tools help define the room. It feels like a place where things grow.
Tanneries
A tannery is a workshop for working leather and materials through water. A cauldron is central to the craft.
Storage for hides and finished goods, water access, and working space define a proper tannery.
Churches
A church is a place of gathering and faith. It may have a brewing stand as part of its quiet hospitality.
Light, quiet, enclosure, and a sense of purpose make a room feel like a place of spiritual gathering.
Room Types & Hints

§6Room Hints§r
Not sure what kind of room to build? Here are hints to guide your search. Rooms are listed from simplest to most complex.

§6The Fundamentals§r
§lBedroom:§r A place of rest. Every room starts with what it sleeps on.
§lStorage:§r Things go in boxes. A room full of containers serves many purposes.

§lKitchen:§r Heat and preparation. Food cooks where fires burn and supplies sit nearby.
§lLibrary:§r Knowledge gathered on shelves. A room can be quiet and thoughtful.

§lWorkshop:§r General purpose crafting. Where many trades can happen.
§lAtelier:§r Alchemy and brewing. A cauldron with a brewing stand nearby.

§6Building Purpose§r
§lSmithing:§r Hot work and repair. Where metal gets shaped and tools get fixed.
§lEnchanting:§r Magic requires study. A table of power surrounded by knowledge.

§lMasonry:§r Stone takes shape. A workshop where stone and skill meet.
§6Specialized Spaces§r
§lMap Room:§r Planning and strategy. Charts, references, and careful layout.

§lLoom Room:§r Textiles need space. Woven materials and organized work.
§lFletchery:§r Precision and ranged work. The place where arrows and bows are made with care.

§6Complex Combinations§r
§lGardener's Shed:§r Plants need tending. Tools, compost, and careful cultivation.
§lGreenhouse:§r Growth behind glass. Light, water, and protection together.

§lTannery:§r Water and materials. A cauldron used alone, without brewing.
§6The Sacred & Uncommon§r
§lChurch:§r Faith and gathering. A quiet place in a community, with simple comforts.
Once you find a room type, its full entry will unlock in this journal. Use these hints to guide your exploration and experiments.
Room Discoveries

§6Progress Tracker§r
Rooms you discover will unlock their full entries in this journal.

Visit each room type's entry to learn more about what makes them special.

(Check the individual room entries above to see what you've discovered!)

message.zen_atelier.journal_tip: Use the Room Journal to inspect your bedroom and learn how to improve your rest.
message.zen_atelier.room_proficiency_1: You've discovered your first room type.
